title: Cardiac Arrest: VF / Pulseless VT
learningObjectives: Describe the immediate management sequence for ventricular fibrillation (VF) and pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT).
State the correct epinephrine dose and timing pattern during VF/pVT arrest.
State the correct antiarrhythmic doses (amiodarone and lidocaine) for refractory VF/pVT.
Explain why chest compressions resume immediately after a shock without a pulse check.
Describe the 2-minute CPR cycle structure and when rhythm/pulse checks occur.
Differentiate first-shock energy strategy from the drug-timing pattern that follows.
Identify when a VF/pVT arrest should prompt consideration of refractory-arrest strategies.

summary.title: Recognizing a Shockable Rhythm
summary.content: Ventricular fibrillation (VF) is chaotic, disorganized electrical activity with no coordinated contraction; pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT) is a rapid, wide-complex rhythm without a palpable pulse. Both are shockable rhythms — the single most important intervention is immediate defibrillation, because survival from a shockable rhythm declines with every minute defibrillation is delayed.

summary.title: The Shock-CPR Cycle
summary.content: Deliver one shock at the manufacturer-recommended (or escalating) biphasic energy, then resume chest compressions immediately without checking a pulse or rhythm. Continue high-quality CPR for about 2 minutes, then briefly pause to check the rhythm (and pulse only if an organized rhythm appears). If still VF/pVT, shock again and repeat the cycle. Minimizing the pre- and post-shock pause is as important as the shock itself.

summary.title: Epinephrine Timing
summary.content: Give epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO as soon as it is reasonably available, typically established after the second shock in a persistent VF/pVT arrest, then repeated every 3–5 minutes for the duration of the arrest. Epinephrine does not interrupt the shock-CPR cycle — it is pushed during ongoing compressions and followed by a saline flush.

summary.title: Antiarrhythmics for Refractory VF/pVT
summary.content: If VF/pVT persists after the initial shocks and epinephrine, give an antiarrhythmic: amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO as the first dose, followed by a second dose of 150 mg IV/IO if VF/pVT continues; or lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg IV/IO as the first dose, followed by 0.5–0.75 mg/kg IV/IO for subsequent doses. Use one agent consistently rather than alternating between them within the same arrest.

summary.title: Maintaining CPR Quality Through the Cycle
summary.content: Throughout every shock-drug-CPR cycle, protect the fundamentals: rate 100–120/min, depth 2–2.4 in (5–6 cm), full recoil, chest compression fraction ≥60%, and a compressor switch about every 2 minutes (conveniently timed with the rhythm check). Waveform capnography, once an advanced airway is placed, helps monitor CPR quality and detect ROSC.

summary.title: When to Consider Refractory-Arrest Strategies
summary.content: If VF/pVT persists despite standard shocks, epinephrine, and an antiarrhythmic (roughly after 3 or more shocks), the arrest is considered refractory. At this point, teams may consider selective strategies such as vector-change or dual sequential defibrillation, or evaluate the patient for ECPR candidacy where systems support it — these are detailed in the Reversible Causes & Refractory Arrest chapter rather than repeated here.

clinicalPearls: Shock, then hands back on the chest immediately — never pause for a pulse check right after a shock.
Epinephrine and antiarrhythmics are pushed during ongoing compressions, not during a special pause.
Use one antiarrhythmic consistently (amiodarone or lidocaine) rather than switching between them mid-arrest.
Every minute of delayed defibrillation in a shockable rhythm measurably lowers survival — prioritize the shock over everything else at the first rhythm check.
redFlags: Long pre-shock or post-shock pauses in compressions.
Withholding or delaying defibrillation to first obtain IV access or place an airway.
Alternating between amiodarone and lidocaine within the same arrest without reason.
commonMistakes: Checking a pulse immediately after every shock instead of resuming compressions.
Giving epinephrine and the antiarrhythmic at the same moment rather than sequencing them across cycles as the rhythm persists.
Losing track of the 2-minute cycle timing and checking rhythm too frequently, adding unnecessary pauses.
memoryAids: 'Shock, then push' — no pulse check right after defibrillation.
Epi every 3–5 min; amiodarone 300 then 150; lidocaine 1–1.5 then 0.5–0.75.
Rhythm check ≈ compressor switch — piggyback the two so you don't add extra pauses.

decisionTrees.title: VF/pVT Arrest Cycle
decisionTrees.content:
VF or pVT confirmed on the monitor
  → Shock immediately (biphasic, manufacturer/escalating energy)
  → Resume CPR immediately, no pulse check → 2 min cycle
  → Rhythm check
     → Still VF/pVT: shock again; give epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO (start ~after 2nd shock, then q3–5 min)
     → Still VF/pVT after next cycle: shock again; give amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO OR lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg IV/IO
     → Still VF/pVT: shock again; second antiarrhythmic dose (amiodarone 150 mg or lidocaine 0.5–0.75 mg/kg); continue epinephrine q3–5 min
     → Persistent VF/pVT after ≥3 shocks: consider refractory-arrest strategies (see Reversible Causes & Refractory Arrest chapter)
     → Organized rhythm appears: check pulse → ROSC pathway or PEA/asystole pathway as appropriate

references: AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Part 9: Adult Advanced Life Support (VF/pVT management)

graphics.caption: Resuscitation flowchart showing CPR, Shock, Epinephrine administration, and Antiarrhythmic administration (Amiodarone/Lidocaine) sequence.
